text: type of ad you come across when you just got your meta ad account banned😂

Saw this and the angle alone is worth saving.  

"Did you know low estrogen can cause micro-tears in your rotator cuff? There is a high concentration of estrogen receptors in the rotator cuff tendons which are responsible for maintaining tendon strength and integrity."  

The hook is a mechanism most readers have never heard. shoulder pain is usually framed as aging or overuse, and the buyer has already been told that by a doctor. introducing estrogen as the missing variable creates a wait-what moment that almost guarantees the next scroll is downward.  

Then the symptom list reads like a diagnostic checklist. outer arm pain, overhead movement pain, clicking, range of motion loss. the buyer who has at least two of those is now mentally raising their hand. you cannot scroll away once you've identified with three of five symptoms in a row.  

The 48% stat anchors the claim in research-flavored language. one number is enough. the moment you stack three or four, the ad starts sounding like an academic paper and the conversion drops.  

The disqualification block is the real conversion engine. it names painkillers, massages, chiropractors, glucosamine, collagen, and explains why each one fails. that is every previous attempt the buyer has made, listed back to them with a single reason they didn't work. by the time provitalize gets introduced, the buyer has already accepted the diagnosis.  

The no-HRT line is the relief valve. it preempts the biggest objection in the menopause supplement space.  been running 24 days.

The ad isn't selling a fungus solution, it's selling access to a secret. Big difference

This brand is running hinglish copy and it's the right call for the audience.  

"Aaj ke oily food, stress & irregular lifestyle ki wajah se acidity, gas, bloating aur constipation common ho chuka hai."  

Mixing hindi and english in the same sentence is how the target buyer actually talks, and ads that match the inner voice of the reader always outperform translated copy. the writer here understood that an indian middle-class buyer scrolling reels at night is not reading polished english, they're reading the way they think.  

The problem stack at the top is broad on purpose. acidity, gas, bloating, constipation. four conditions covered in one sentence widens the audience without diluting the offer, because the underlying product is a digestion juice that targets all four.  

The ayurvedic ingredient list does the credibility work. amla, harad, baheda, sonth, pipali. these are heritage words that a north-indian buyer recognizes from childhood. you cannot fake that vocabulary. it signals that the brand is rooted in tradition, not imported from a us supplement template.  

The second-half pivot to lightness and energy is the aspirational close. relief is the floor, but feeling energetic through the day is the ceiling the buyer is actually after. naming both gives the ad a wider emotional reach than a pure relief pitch.  

Format choice with the green leaf, the cart emoji, the structured checkmarks reads like a vernacular feed-native ad, not a brand template.

The best health ads don't educate first, they make the right person feel seen
bro's meta ads knowledge after reading this:

Scrolling and this one stopped me with the testimonial choice.  

"60% OFF Sale Ends Today! Relieved my carpal tunnel within 2 weeks. 15 mins 3 times daily. Also helps with arthritis. Jim F."  

The structure is doing three jobs at once. urgency on top, social proof in the middle, guarantee at the bottom. that's a complete buying frame in twenty words, no fluff, no setup.  

What makes the testimonial work is the specificity. two weeks. fifteen minutes. three times daily. real numbers from a real-sounding name. fake testimonials almost always avoid concrete protocols because they don't know what the product actually requires. jim's testimonial reads like he actually used it.  

The also-helps-with-arthritis tag at the end of the quote is sneaky-good. it expands the addressable audience without making the ad about arthritis. anyone scrolling who has hand pain that isn't carpal tunnel now has a reason to keep reading.  

The 90-day guarantee at the bottom is the safety net that lets the buyer click on a name they've never heard of before. free shipping plus refund flips the risk back onto the brand, which is the only way unknown ecom brands move impulse purchases in a pain category.  

Sale ends today is the oldest trick in the book and it still works because the buyer knows they'll forget by tomorrow.

Found this local hospital ad and it's a useful study in trust-stacking for healthcare.  

"15+ Yrs experienced surgeons. 60000+ Happy Patients. Lower complications & higher success rates. All Insurance Accepted. 0% Interest EMI. End to end patient care. Free pick & drop services."  

Every line is a single objection neutralized. experience covers competence doubt. 60,000 patients cover volume doubt. insurance and emi cover cost doubt. pickup covers logistics doubt. the ad reads like a checklist of every reason someone would not call, with a counter for each one.  

The phone number sitting in the hook is the actual conversion driver. healthcare ads that try to push a form-fill underperform brands that just put a number at the top. the older buyer dialing from a parked car is the actual audience, not someone filling out a multi-step quiz.  

Minimally invasive in the headline is the product positioning. kidney stones is the search term. bangalore is the geo. all three keywords land in the first sentence, which is how this thing keeps reaching the right intent.  

The green checkmarks do scanning work. on a phone feed, that visual pattern makes the bullets feel like guarantees instead of features. small format choice, real lift on read-through.  

Healthcare creatives rarely need to be clever. they need to remove friction and signal volume. this one does both without wasting a word.  

Been running 32 days.

This hit different because it opens at the surgery line and works backwards.  

"I genuinely thought I was heading toward surgery. The pain on the outside of my hip got so bad I couldn't sleep on that side anymore. I had to put a pillow between my knees just to get through the night."  

Stakes first. surgery is the worst-case outcome the reader is privately fearing and naming it in the first sentence creates immediate identification. the pillow between the knees detail does what generic pain copy can never do. it's a specific behavior every menopausal woman with hip pain has tried.  

Then the unmasking. gluteal tendinopathy gets named. estrogen support for tendons gets explained. an OB-GYN delivers the line that previous doctors missed. that handoff from suffering to diagnosis is the spine of the entire ad.  the failed-attempts list is the proof. turmeric, collagen, painkillers, physio. she rules out every cheaper option before introducing the product, which preempts the buyer's i already tried that objection.  

The product mention comes late and sounds like a tip from a friend, not a pitch. moringa phytoestrogens with curcumin. specific ingredient pairing, named brand, no superlatives. that restraint is what makes it read like a story instead of a sales letter.  

The last line about not imagining it is doing validation work that converts. women in this market have been gaslit by their own doctors for years.  been running 15 days.

The order of the proof points is probably more important than most people realize.

Another one in the hearing space and the brevity is the whole strategy.  

"Say goodbye to expensive hearing aids 👇"  

That is the entire ad body. six words and an emoji pointing at the click. on paper it looks lazy. in practice it's a category-specific trick that converts because of who it targets.  

The buyer here is older, often spending more time on facebook than reading. long copy filters them out. short copy with a price-anger angle gets the tap before doubt sets in. expensive hearing aids is the universal complaint of anyone who has shopped this category and walked out of a clinic angry.  

What's smart is the absence of a product name in the hook. there's nothing to google, nothing to compare, nothing to bounce on. the only path forward is the link. that funnels every curious scroll into the landing page where the real pitch lives.  

The format choice with a video creative is doing the heavy lifting the copy refuses to. you don't need a body when the video is showing the device, the price, and the testimonial. the body just needs to not get in the way.  

This is the kind of ad that proves long copy isn't always the move. when the angle is rage at the current solution, you only need to name the rage and point at the door.  been running 26 days.

Spotted this in the gout space and the writing is doing real work in a category that usually sounds clinical.  

"Calm Flares: ease the throbbing, swelling, and bedsheet-can't-touch-it pain. Clear Uric Acid at the Source, not just mask it at the surface."  

The magic word in there is bedsheet-can't-touch-it. every gout sufferer knows exactly what that means. it's a sensory detail nobody who hasn't lived through it would write. that one phrase does what twenty bullet points could not. it tells the reader the copywriter understands the condition.  

The three-bullet structure is also smart. calm flares now, clear uric acid at the source, prevent future flare-ups. that's a present, root cause, and future story all in one frame. it answers the three questions a gout buyer is silently asking when they scroll.  

The cold-processed and anthocyanins mentions give the science buyer something to hang onto. they don't overdose the reader on biochemistry, just enough vocabulary to signal that the product was built, not blended.  

The cherry emoji as the bullet marker is a soft touch. it carries the visual brand without taking up real estate. small detail, but it makes the ad scan faster than a standard bullet list.  

Introducing goodgrove as the product name late in the copy is also worth noting. the buyer reads the symptom relief first, the mechanism second, and the brand third. that ordering lets the offer feel like a solution they discovered, not a pitch they tolerated.  been running 52 days.

That opener basically forces the reader to ask, "Wait... am I using dial-up?"

Ran into this BFCM creative and i'm impressed it's still in market. b2b ads with this much personality are rare.  

"Stock up on Black Friday & Cyber Monday assets that drop harder than the prices: Snappy icons, illustrations, Lottie animations and 3D illustrations that make every promo feel irresistible."  

The audience here is designers and marketing teams scrambling for promo art in q4. the ad knows that and writes in their dialect. drop harder than the prices is the kind of line a creative director would actually say out loud, which is why it lands with the people it's targeting.  

What i like is the bundle framing. icons plus illustrations plus lottie plus 3d in one breath. that's positioning iconscout as a one-stop shop without ever using the phrase one-stop shop. four asset types named in a single sentence does more than a bullet list would.  

The second half does the emotional job. make every promo feel irresistible speaks to the actual fear of the buyer. nobody buys stock assets because they want stock assets. they buy because they're stressed about a campaign deadline and need their landing page to look finished by friday.  

Format-wise, animated assets in the creative itself is the smartest move. show the product working, don't describe it. a static ad for an animation library would be self-defeating.  been running 204 days.

The best podcast ads aren't selling products. They're telling stories with a product attached.
Most brands don't need a new format. They need more iterations of a format that's already proven.

Caught my eye because the opening reframes blame in one move. nobody wants to be told they failed.  

"You are not doing it wrong. Your probiotic just was not built for the full job."  

That's the whole psychological lever. permission first, then product. the buyer who has tried three probiotics with no result was already loyal to the idea, just frustrated with the execution. this ad hands them an explanation that doesn't make them feel stupid.  

Then the breakdown does the actual selling. one strain instead of hundreds. no prebiotics. no postbiotics. no enzymes. each gap is a reason the previous purchase underdelivered, and conveniently each gap maps to a feature on this label.  

The phrase about one piece of the solution with three others left open is the kind of line that gets quoted in screenshots. it compresses a five-bullet argument into one image of an incomplete puzzle. that's memorable in a way feature lists never are.  

The David Beckham co-founder mention is doing celebrity-trust work without leaning on it as the headline. burying it as the third item is confident positioning. brands that lead with celebrity sound desperate. brands that drop it after the science sound legitimate.  

90 ingredients, 90-day guarantee, one scoop. the symmetry of those numbers makes the whole thing feel like a finished product, not a stack of supplements.

Why the f*** are you sharing this??

Here's what makes this nattokinase ad sticky for me. it teaches before it sells.  

"Many brands advertise 8,000 FU on the front, but quietly split that dose across 3 to 4 capsules. You think you're taking a clinical dose. You're actually getting 2,000 FU."  

That is a hostile claim against the category and it's specific enough to be checkable. specificity is what makes hostile claims work. vague villains don't convert, named mechanics do.  

Then it stacks proofs. 10,800 FU per serving. no blends. third-party tested. cGMP. two capsules equals full dose. a buyer scanning this for the first time reads it as they did the math i can't be bothered to do.  

The closer is dose-dependent reasoning. they explain why concentration matters before they ask for the click. that one paragraph turns a price objection into a logic problem, and most readers will resolve the logic in the brand's favor.  

Format is long copy on a video creative, which is rare. it bets that the right buyer wants more information, not less. when the audience is cholesterol-aware and family-history-driven, that bet usually pays.  the 

Closing line about placebo math is also worth stealing. it implies that competitors aren't just underdosing, they're banking on the customer not noticing. that's a stronger accusation than calling them weak, and it positions the brand as the one company that respects the buyer's intelligence.  been running 18 days.

This one leans hard on origin story and i think it earns every word of it.  

"If Viking women had a skincare secret, this was it. Found only in high-altitude Nordic fjords, wild Sea Buckthorn was prized by Arctic women for healing wounds, restoring circulation, and keeping skin resilient through frost and wind."  

The hook is a transportation device. it lifts the buyer out of a noisy feed and drops them into a cold fjord with a clear cultural image. vikings, arctic, frost, wind. you can feel the climate in two sentences.  

Then the ingredient story does double duty. sea buckthorn isn't a familiar word so it sounds rare. high-altitude and high-latitude are scarcity signals. and the function list ties the romance to results. wound healing and circulation are old-world claims that bypass the usual hydration vocabulary every other skincare brand uses.  

The negative list is the close. no water, no fragrance, no preservatives. each "no" is a competitor disqualified by category. and the smell-and-feel line is sensory writing that lands without sounding like marketing.  

The 60-day guarantee at the bottom is the proof. saga up top, refund at the bottom, the whole thing balances mystique with safety.  

The audience is mid-to-late thirties women who have stopped responding to mainstream skincare and are looking for something with a story they can repeat to a friend. nordic, wild, ancestral. those are share-worthy descriptors that a buyer will use in a text message when recommending the product.
